summary: Gruber analyzes Jony Ive's promotion to Chief Design Officer, weighing whether it's a genuine elevation or the beginning of his departure from Apple. He examines Ben Thompson's characterization of the announcement as 'masterful public relations' and the suspicious detail that Ive will 'travel more.' After considering the longstanding speculation about Ive wanting to return to England, Gruber concludes that the most likely reading is that Ive has been promoted to a Steve Jobs-like role as Apple's arbiter of taste, with his lieutenants Alan Dye and Richard Howarth handling day-to-day management. He notes the near-unprecedented nature of a new C-level title at Apple, suggesting Cook-Ive mirrors the Jobs-Cook leadership duo in reverse.
key_points: The announcement was orchestrated through planted seeds in prior press profiles of Dye and Howarth, then timed for a holiday — a masterful PR operation

Alan Dye is polarizing within Apple not for personality reasons but because his graphic design background pushed iOS toward 'flat' design, which some veteran UI designers consider harmful to usability
The 'travel more' detail likely refers to Ive spending time in England, but the window for his family to relocate has effectively closed with his twins already being educated in California
Ive's new role essentially mirrors Steve Jobs's position as overseer and arbiter of taste across all Apple touchpoints, from hardware to retail to packaging
Apple has only created three C-level positions in the modern era (CEO, COO, CDO), making this title almost unprecedented and unlikely to be merely ceremonial
money_quotes: A 'masterful display of public relations' feels exactly right. With one exception, though: clarifying the degree of Ive's ongoing involvement in Apple's design work.
What makes for good graphic design does not necessarily make for good user interface design, and often makes for bad user interface design.
Is this truly a promotion for Ive, or is it (as Thompson punctuated it) a quote-unquote 'promotion'?
I don't ever expect Jony Ive to drop the 'aluminium', but he and his family are Californians.
A title can just be a title, but Apple has only had three C-level executives in the modern era.
Tim Cook doesn't strike me as being big on ceremony.
It's hard not to read Cook's description of Ive's responsibilities as pretty much matching those of Steve Jobs while he was CEO.
Cook oversees operations and 'running the company'; Ive oversees everything else.
key_insight: Ive's promotion to CDO most likely represents a genuine elevation to a Jobs-like arbiter-of-taste role across all of Apple, not a graceful exit — but only time will tell.
